build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: Story is delivered as paced narration/memory text the player drives forward (clicking or a key reveals the next line) over recognizable room, object, or memory scenes, rather than dumping all text at once or auto-scrolling. Strong submissions integrate narration with visible keepsakes, scene changes, and reflective framing; a mostly empty screen with a text box should be judged low even if the text advances correctly. Score 0 if there is no readable advancing text system, if text appears all at once with no pacing, or if the player cannot control progression.

Examining keepsakes is an actual player action with free choice of order: the player selects which object to take up from a room/box of several, the chosen keepsake is presented (recognizable illustrated item plus the memory it reveals), and the player can return and pick others in an order they control rather than a fixed slideshow. Object selection should be visually grounded in the room or box, not just a list of text buttons. Score 0 if there is nothing to examine, if objects cannot be selected, or if examination is just linear advance with no player choice of what to look at.

Each examined keepsake records a persistent fragment into the player's gathered knowledge, and that collection is visible (a list, journal, or memory board) and grows as objects are examined. The game tracks what has and has not been found. Score 0 if examined objects are not recorded, if the gathered fragments are never shown, or if examining leaves no tracked state.

The fragments gate later content: which interpretations, lines, or endings become available depends on which keepsakes the player examined. A later beat must demonstrably depend on an earlier discovery — an understanding only offered to a player who found a particular fragment, or an ending unlocked by a set of fragments — not just the most recent click. Score 0 if fragments never affect anything beyond a list on screen, if all content is reachable regardless of what was examined, or if the experience is effectively linear.

The full loop is connected without restarting the executable: title -> the task begins -> examine keepsakes and reach interpretation choices -> arrive at a closing understanding (ending) -> return to title or begin again to reach a different one. Every transition is reachable through in-game UI, and reaching an ending halts progression on a styled result screen. Score 0 if any transition is missing or broken, if the piece cannot reach an ending, or if replaying requires relaunching the application.

There are several distinct keepsakes to examine, each a different object with its own illustrated presentation and its own fragment of the past — a photograph, a letter, a ring, a diary, and so on — not reskins of one generic item. Different keepsakes should reveal different pieces of the life through specific memories, not interchangeable labels or repeated text. Score 0 if there is only one object or a single repeated item.

Fragments genuinely connect and recontextualize: at least one later fragment changes the meaning of an earlier one (a date explains a photo, an absence answers a question), and the order or combination the player examines materially affects the understanding they assemble. Connections should change how the player reads earlier material, not just add one isolated sentence. Score 0 if fragments are independent and never inform one another, or if there is only one fixed reading.

The piece reaches multiple distinct closing understandings (a life redeemed, a secret kept in kindness, a quiet grief, a truth that recasts everything, etc.) meaningfully different in meaning and tied to which fragments the player found and how they read them. At least two different endings are observable across the demos as authored conclusions reached through different discovery paths, not isolated debug screens or the same ending retitled. Score 0 if there is only one possible ending or no real ending.

The reconstructed life has narrative breadth: the keepsakes together sketch a real person across more than one episode or relationship — youth, love, a falling-out, a regret — and interpretation choices pose genuinely different ways to read the dead person's actions, so there is varied material rather than one short memory repeated. Extra fragments should carry distinct episode, voice, and interpretive weight. Score 0 if there is only a single short fragment with no breadth.

The writing delivers an authored, emotionally resonant reconstruction: the memories have specific texture and voice, the connections land with feeling, and the buried secret carries real weight. The content should read as sustained authored prose with scene texture, emotional escalation, and reflection; a few strong isolated lines should not carry an otherwise thin demo. Score 0 if there is no actual narrative content or only lorem-style placeholder text.

Core reading UI is clear and stable at 1280x720: narration/memory text is legible in a framed box or panel, the keepsakes available to examine are clearly presented and selectable, and the collected-fragments display is readable. Nothing critical overlaps or is clipped. Score 0 if text, the keepsake choices, or the fragment list are unreadable, overlapping, or clipped.

Player input produces clear, immediate feedback: selecting a keepsake visibly opens it and reveals its memory, examining it visibly adds to the collected fragments, an interpretation choice shows a selected state and is acknowledged, and advancing a line changes the text, object view, memory scene, or fragment state. Feedback limited to instant text replacement in the same static layout should be judged low. Score 0 if input produces no visible response, or if it is impossible to tell that an examination or choice registered.

Structure is legible: the player can tell where they are — title vs. the room/selection vs. examining a keepsake vs. an interpretation choice vs. ending — and transitions are clear and unhurried rather than abrupt jumps with no framing. Endings read unmistakably as endings with their own authored presentation. Score 0 if phases are visually indistinguishable or transitions are confusing.

Overall visual style is coherent and authored across title, room, keepsakes, and endings: a consistent quiet, melancholy art direction with a deliberate muted palette, gentle typography, and framing. The game should look like a published narrative game, not a debug screen of text and buttons. Strong submissions rely on real illustrated or pixel-art treatment; a scene built mainly from ColorRects, circles, silhouettes, and flat primitive shapes should be judged low even if the colors are coherent. Score 0 if the screen is dominated by default Godot widgets, untextured rectangles, or placeholder text.

Backgrounds and keepsakes use real illustrated or pixel-art assets: the room/box has an illustrated backdrop, and each keepsake is shown as a recognizable illustrated object (a photo, a letter, a ring) rather than a bare colored shape or a text label alone. Different keepsakes look visibly different. Bare rectangles, simple icons, empty rooms, or geometric stand-ins should receive little credit. Score 0 if scenes have no background art and keepsakes are plain rectangles/labels.

The narration box, the collected-fragments journal/board, the keepsake selection UI, choice menus, title, and ending screens use styled, themed UI — illustrated frames, soft panels, custom or styled fonts fitting the tender memorial mood — rather than raw default Godot Label/Button/Panel defaults or plain flat boxes. UI polish should make the game feel like an intimate memory interface, not just readable text containers. Score 0 if primary UI is obviously unstyled default widgets.

Presentation has gentle motion and polish appropriate to the mood: text reveals progressively, picking up a keepsake animates (a fade, a soft zoom, the object lifting into view), fragments are added with a quiet flourish, and scene transitions use soft fades rather than instant hard cuts, with unhurried atmosphere (drifting dust, warm light). Motion should support the mood beyond text appearing in a box. Score 0 if nothing on screen ever animates and every change is an instant snap.
